title: Psalms 70
subtitle: Hasten, O God, to Save Me

sections.header: Verses 1–3 — “May Those Who Seek My Life Be Put to Shame”

heb.paragraph: "Hasten (ḥūshâ), O God, to save me; come quickly, LORD, to help me" (v.1). Nearly identical to Psalm 40:13–17 — preserved independently as an urgent prayer. The brevity IS the urgency: five verses, no preamble, no argument. Just: hurry.

notes.note: "Hasten, O God, to save me." No introduction, no backstory. Straight to the emergency. The psalm is a compressed SOS.
"May those who seek my life be put to shame. May those who say ‘Aha!’ be turned back in disgrace." The enemies gloat with "Aha!" — the taunt of the triumphant bully.

refs.note: This psalm is nearly identical to Psalm 40:13-17, extracted for independent liturgical use.
The same prayer that enemies be turned back in disgrace appears in multiple Davidic psalms.
The immediately following psalm echoes the urgent 'do not be far from me, come quickly.'

hist.context: Psalm 70 is a brief urgent prayer for deliverance, nearly identical to Psalm 40:13-17 but adapted as a separate liturgy. The superscription 'for remembrance' (lehazkir) may indicate use with memorial offerings. The brevity suggests use in crisis when lengthy prayer is impossible — a concentrated cry for immediate help.

sections.header: Verses 4–5 — “I Am Poor and Needy; Come Quickly”

heb.paragraph: "But as for me, I am poor and needy (ʿānî); come quickly to me, O God. You are my help and my deliverer; LORD, do not delay" (v.5). The psalm’s last words: "do not delay." The prayer is bookended by urgency: "hasten" (v.1) and "do not delay" (v.5). Between them: poverty acknowledged, help claimed, deliverance expected.

notes.note: "May all who seek you rejoice and be glad in you. May those who long for your saving help always say, ‘The LORD is great!’" A brief prayer for the community inserted into the personal emergency.
"I am poor and needy. Come quickly to me, O God. Do not delay." Self-description: poor. Theology: God is help and deliverer. Request: don’t delay. The entire psalm in one verse.

refs.note: Rejoicing in the Lord always echoes the psalm's prayer that seekers 'rejoice and be glad.'
Those who desire the vindication of the righteous saying 'the LORD be exalted' parallels this psalm.
God bringing justice speedily for those who cry to him answers the psalm's urgent petition.

hist.context: The closing self-description 'poor and needy' (ʿānî weʾebyôn) is characteristic of the Psalter's piety — identifying with the vulnerable rather than the powerful. The request for God to 'not delay' (v.5) maintains the psalm's note of urgency throughout. This psalm models compressed prayer for those in extremity who cannot elaborate their petitions.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: One in urgent need of immediate divine rescue (vv.1-5)
ppl.text: This brief psalm (5 verses) is nearly identical to Psalm 40:13-17 — extracted as a standalone urgent prayer. The opening cry 'Hasten, O God, to save me! Come quickly, LORD, to help me!' (v.1) was adopted by the early church as the versicle opening fixed-hour prayer (the preces). David is 'poor and needy' (v.5) — royal status offers no protection without divine intervention. The psalm's brevity is its power: no theological argument, no recounting of past mercies, just desperate, undiluted petition.

ppl.name: God (Elohim)
ppl.role: The urgently needed deliverer and helper (vv.1,5)
ppl.text: David addresses God as both 'deliverer' (natsal — to snatch away from danger) and 'helper' (azar — to surround with aid). The psalm's request is pure speed: 'do not delay' (v.5). While other psalms build elaborate cases for why God should act, Psalm 70 simply screams for help — the theology is implicit in the act of crying out. The contrast between David's enemies who 'seek my life' (v.2) and those who 'seek you' (v.4) frames the conflict as ultimately between those oriented toward God and those oriented against him.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVBut as for me, I am poor and needy; come quickly to me, O God. You are my help and my deliverer; Lord, do not delay.ESVBut I am poor and needy; hasten to me, O God! You are my help and my deliverer; O LORD, do not delay!NIVMay those who want to take my life be put to shame and confusion; may all who desire my ruin be turned back in disgrace.ESVLet them be put to shame and confusion who seek my life! Let them be turned back and brought to dishonor who delight in my hurt!

rec.text: ""Hasten, O God, to save me." No introduction, no backstory. Straight to the emergency. The psalm is a compressed SOS."

rows.label: The Emergency — 70:1–3
rows.text: "Hasten, O God!" No preamble. Pure urgency. "Aha!" turned to shame.

hebtext.note: "Hasten (ḥūshâ), O God, to save me; come quickly, LORD, to help me" (v.1). Nearly identical to Psalm 40:13–17 — preserved independently as an urgent prayer. The brevity IS the urgency: five verses, no preamble, no argument. Just: hurry.

"But as for me, I am poor and needy (ʿānî); come quickly to me, O God. You are my help and my deliverer; LORD, do not delay" (v.5). The psalm’s last words: "do not delay." The prayer is bookended by urgency: "hasten" (v.1) and "do not delay" (v.5). Between them: poverty acknowledged, help claimed, deliverance expected.

themes.note: Faith: urgency as prayer form. The briefest psalm in this section — urgency stripped of everything but the plea.

prayer_prompt: As you reflect on this urgent, breathless plea — 'Hasten, O God, to save me' — consider asking God whether you bring Him your emergencies with this kind of raw immediacy, or whether you compose yourself first and lose the honesty.
